title: Habits
description: Build small habits, one tick at a time. Influenced by James Clear's Atomic Habits.

cta.deleteConfirm: Delete this habit and all its ticks? This can't be undone.

home.title: Habits
home.subtitle: Small habits, repeated. Tap to tick - no streaks, no shame.
home.noHabitsBody: Atomic Habits suggests scaling new behaviours under two minutes. Pick something small - the goal is to cast a vote for the kind of person you want to become.
home.recentEmpty: Ticks you make will appear here.
home.neverMissTwiceBody: You skipped yesterday. Today is a great day to tick this once - one missed day is data, not failure.

list.tapToAvoid: Tap to mark you avoided this today

form.identityPlaceholder: I'm the kind of person who…
form.identityHelp: Every habit is a vote for the kind of person you want to become.
form.nameRequired: Give the habit a short name.
form.kindBuildHelp: A new behaviour you want to cast votes for.
form.kindBreakHelp: A behaviour you want to do less. Inverse the four laws - invisible, unattractive, difficult, unsatisfying.
form.twoMinutePlaceholder: Make the starter version under two minutes. e.g., Read one page.
form.twoMinuteHelp: Scale the habit down so it's easy to start.
form.difficultPlaceholder: Add steps that slow the behaviour. e.g., Log out after every session.
form.difficultHelp: Increase friction so the unwanted action takes more effort than ignoring it.
form.invisiblePlaceholder: Remove or hide the trigger. e.g., Put the phone in another room while reading.
form.unattractivePlaceholder: Name the cost. e.g., Doom-scrolling steals an hour of my evening.
form.unsatisfyingPlaceholder: Add a small immediate consequence. e.g., Tell a friend; pay a forfeit.
form.rewardPlaceholder: How will you mark this as a small win?
form.customDaysRequired: Pick at least one day for a custom cadence.
form.saveError: Couldn't save the habit. Try again.
form.draftRestored: Draft restored from your previous edit.

detail.calendarEmpty: No ticks recorded yet. Tap any day to tick it.
detail.notesEmpty: Notes you save with a tick will appear here.
detail.archiveConfirmBody: Archived habits leave today's list but keep their ticks for history.

log.title: Add a note
log.subtitle: Optional reflection for the day's tick. Stay private - only you see this.
log.saveError: Couldn't save the note. Try again.
log.missingTick: Tick the day first to attach a note.

history.title: History
history.subtitle: Every tick you've made, newest first.
history.empty: Once you tick a habit, that day will appear here.

welcome.title: Small habits, remarkable results
welcome.subtitle: Influenced by James Clear's Atomic Habits.
welcome.compoundingBody: Improving by 1% a day compounds to ~37× better in a year. Drifting 1% worse a day collapses toward zero. Small consistent actions - not heroic effort - move the curve.
welcome.systemsBody: "You do not rise to the level of your goals; you fall to the level of your systems." This tool captures the daily action, not an outcome target.

loop.title: The habit loop & four laws
loop.subtitle: Every habit cycles through cue, craving, response, reward. Four design rules attach to each stage.
loop.obviousBody: Design a clear cue. "I will read at 8pm in the living room."
loop.attractiveBody: Bundle the new habit with something you already enjoy.
loop.easyBody: Scale it under two minutes. Friction is the enemy of consistency.
loop.satisfyingBody: Mark the win. Visible progress reinforces the loop.
loop.breakNote: To break a habit, invert each law - make it invisible, unattractive, difficult, and unsatisfying.

identity.title: Identity-based habits
identity.subtitle: Durable change is identity change.
identity.body: Instead of "I want to run a marathon," frame it as "I'm a runner." Every tick is a vote for that identity. The habit form has an optional identity field - leave it blank if you'd rather not name one.

neverMiss.title: Never miss twice & the two-minute rule
neverMiss.subtitle: Two ideas that keep practice non-punitive.
neverMiss.twoMinuteBody: Scale every habit down so the starter version takes under two minutes. "Read one page," not "Read for an hour."
neverMiss.neverMissBody: Missing once is data. Missing twice starts a new habit. Selftend will never punish a missed day - we'll just quietly note when you're at risk of missing twice.

finish.error: Couldn't save your preferences. Try again.

errors.toggle: Couldn't update the tick. Try again.
errors.delete: Couldn't delete the habit. Try again.

insights.title: Insights
insights.rhythmEmpty: Tick a habit to see your rhythm.
insights.identityEmpty: Add an identity to a habit to see ticks roll up here.
insights.twoMinuteSubtitle_one: {{filled}} of {{total}} habit has a two-minute starter version.
insights.twoMinuteSubtitle_other: {{filled}} of {{total}} habits have a two-minute starter version.
insights.twoMinuteEmpty: Add a build habit to see this.

learn.indexSubtitle: Ten short cards covering the framework. Read them in any order.

compounding.title: The 1% compounding effect
compounding.short: Tiny improvements compound - for better or for worse.
compounding.body: Improving by 1% a day compounds to roughly 37× better in a year (1.01^365). Drifting 1% worse a day collapses toward zero (0.99^365 ≈ 0.03). Small consistent actions, not heroic effort, move the curve. The lesson: prize consistency over intensity.

systems-over-goals.title: Systems over goals
systems-over-goals.short: You fall to the level of your systems.
systems-over-goals.body: Goals describe an outcome you want; systems describe the daily process that produces it. You do not rise to the level of your goals - you fall to the level of your systems. Selftend captures the daily action (the system) rather than a target number, because the action is what compounds.

habit-loop.title: The habit loop
habit-loop.body: Every habit cycles through four stages: a cue notices a trigger, a craving generates motivation, a response performs the behaviour, and a reward closes the loop. Each of the four laws is a design rule attached to one stage.

make-obvious.title: 1st law - Make it obvious
make-obvious.short: Design a cue you cannot miss.
make-obvious.body: Behaviours start with a cue. Two strategies make the cue obvious: implementation intentions ("I will [behaviour] at [time] in [location]") and habit stacking ("After [current habit], I will [new habit]"). Both pin the new habit to a specific moment instead of vague intention.

make-attractive.title: 2nd law - Make it attractive
make-attractive.short: Borrow anticipation from something you already enjoy.
make-attractive.body: Cravings are dopamine-driven. Temptation bundling pairs an action you want with one you need - only watch the show while folding laundry, only podcast while walking. Joining a culture where the habit is the norm also amplifies the pull.

make-easy.title: 3rd law - Make it easy
make-easy.short: Reduce friction so starting is trivial.
make-easy.body: Habits stop at friction. Lay out the gym clothes the night before. Pre-portion the snack. Disable the notification. The fewer steps between you and the behaviour, the more reliably the habit fires.

make-satisfying.title: 4th law - Make it satisfying
make-satisfying.short: Reinforce the action with an immediate win.
make-satisfying.body: What is immediately rewarded gets repeated; what is immediately punished gets avoided. Visible progress - ticking a box, marking a calendar, sliding a paperclip - provides the small reward the brain needs. The longer-term benefits matter, but immediate satisfaction is what closes the loop.

two-minute-rule.title: The two-minute rule
two-minute-rule.short: Scale every new habit to under two minutes.
two-minute-rule.body: When a new habit feels hard, you've made it too big. "Read before bed" becomes "Read one page." "Run a marathon" becomes "Put on the running shoes." The two-minute version is not the destination - it's the gateway. Once the ritual is automatic, the duration can grow.

never-miss-twice.title: Never miss twice
never-miss-twice.short: Missing once is data. Missing twice is a new habit.
never-miss-twice.body: Perfection is unrealistic; consistency is not. The rule isn't "never miss" - it's "never miss twice." When you slip, the priority is to do the habit again the next day, even at the two-minute size. Selftend never punishes a missed day; it just quietly notes when you're at risk of missing twice.

identity.title: Identity-based habits
identity.short: Every habit is a vote for the kind of person you want to become.
identity.body: Outcome-based goals ("I want to lose weight") fade. Identity-based habits ("I am someone who moves every day") stick, because every action either reinforces or weakens the identity. The aim isn't to perform a habit once; it's to become the kind of person for whom the habit is natural.
